questions.stem: The moment of inertia of a thin uniform rod of mass $M$ and length $L$ about an axis perpendicular to the rod through its centre is
questions.explanation: Integrating $\int x^2\,dm$ along the rod with the origin at the centre, where $x$ runs from $-L/2$ to $L/2$ and the linear density is $M/L$, gives $I = \frac{M}{L}\int_{-L/2}^{L/2} x^2\,dx = \frac{M}{L}\cdot\frac{L^3}{12} = \frac{1}{12}ML^2$. The value $\frac{1}{3}ML^2$ is the moment of inertia about an axis through one end, which is larger because more mass lies far from the axis.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: The parallel axis theorem relates the moment of inertia $I$ about any axis to the moment of inertia $I_{\text{cm}}$ about a parallel axis through the centre of mass, a distance $d$ away. It states
questions.explanation: The parallel axis theorem is $I = I_{\text{cm}} + Md^2$: the moment of inertia about a displaced axis exceeds the central one by $M$ times the square of the offset distance. The added term is always positive, so the centre-of-mass axis gives the smallest moment of inertia among all parallel axes. The distance enters squared, not linearly, and with no factor of one half.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: The moment of inertia of a uniform solid sphere of mass $M$ and radius $R$ about a diameter is
questions.explanation: A uniform solid sphere has moment of inertia $\frac{2}{5}MR^2$ about any diameter. The mass is distributed throughout the volume, with much of it near the centre, so the coefficient $2/5$ is smaller than the $2/3$ of a thin spherical shell, where all the mass sits at radius $R$. The values $\frac{1}{2}MR^2$ and $MR^2$ belong to a solid cylinder and a thin hoop respectively.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: A uniform solid disk (or cylinder) of mass $M$ and radius $R$ rotates about its central symmetry axis. Its moment of inertia is
questions.explanation: For a uniform disk about its central axis, integrating $\int r^2\,dm$ over the area with surface density $M/(\pi R^2)$ gives $I = \frac{1}{2}MR^2$. A thin hoop of the same mass and radius has $I = MR^2$, twice as large, because all its mass is at the rim rather than spread from the centre out. The value $\frac{1}{4}MR^2$ would describe a disk rotating about a diameter in its own plane.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: A uniform thin hoop (ring) of mass $M$ and radius $R$ rotates about the axis through its centre perpendicular to its plane. Its moment of inertia is
questions.explanation: Every element of a thin hoop lies at the same distance $R$ from the central axis, so $I = \int R^2\,dm = R^2\int dm = MR^2$. This is the largest moment of inertia among standard bodies of a given mass and radius, precisely because none of the mass is closer to the axis. A solid disk of the same mass and radius has only half this value.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: The rotational kinetic energy of a rigid body rotating with angular speed $\omega$ about a fixed axis with moment of inertia $I$ is
questions.explanation: Summing $\tfrac{1}{2}\,dm\,v^2$ over the body with $v = r\omega$ gives $T = \tfrac{1}{2}\omega^2\int r^2\,dm = \tfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2$. The form mirrors the translational $\tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$, with the moment of inertia replacing the mass and the angular speed replacing the linear speed. The angular speed is squared and the coefficient is one half; dropping the square or the half gives the wrong energy.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: The moment of inertia of a thin uniform rod of mass $M$ and length $L$ about a perpendicular axis through one end is
questions.explanation: Use the parallel axis theorem with $I_{\text{cm}} = \frac{1}{12}ML^2$ and the end a distance $d = L/2$ from the centre: $I = \frac{1}{12}ML^2 + M\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{12}ML^2 + \frac{1}{4}ML^2 = \frac{1}{12}ML^2 + \frac{3}{12}ML^2 = \frac{1}{3}ML^2$. Direct integration from $0$ to $L$ gives the same $\frac{1}{3}ML^2$. The end axis has a larger moment of inertia than the central one because more mass is far from it.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: The angular momentum $\mathbf{L}$ of a rigid body and its angular velocity $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ are related by the inertia tensor $\mathbf{I}$ through $L_i = \sum_j I_{ij}\omega_j$. In general, $\mathbf{L}$ and $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ are
options: parallel only when $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ lies along a principal axis (or the inertia tensor is isotropic)
questions.explanation: Because the inertia tensor is a matrix, multiplying $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ by it generally rotates the direction, so $\mathbf{L}$ and $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ point differently. They are parallel only when $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ lies along a principal axis, where $\mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega} = I\boldsymbol{\omega}$ with $I$ the corresponding principal moment (an eigenvalue), or trivially when all three principal moments are equal. The principal axes are the eigenvectors of the inertia tensor, the directions in which the body spins cleanly.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: Euler's equations describe rigid-body rotation in the body frame using the principal moments $I_1, I_2, I_3$. The first of the three is
questions.explanation: Euler's first equation is $I_1\dot{\omega}_1 - (I_2 - I_3)\omega_2\omega_3 = N_1$, with the other two following by cyclic permutation of the indices $1\to 2\to 3$. The coupling term involves the difference of the other two principal moments times the product of the other two angular-velocity components, and it is subtracted. These nonlinear coupling terms, arising because the body frame rotates, are what make free rigid-body motion (such as a tumbling object) nontrivial.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: A uniform solid sphere has mass $M = 5\ \text{kg}$ and radius $R = 2\ \text{m}$. What is its moment of inertia about a diameter?
questions.explanation: Using $I = \frac{2}{5}MR^2$ with $M = 5\ \text{kg}$ and $R = 2\ \text{m}$, the radius squared is $R^2 = 4\ \text{m}^2$, so $I = \frac{2}{5}\times 5 \times 4 = \frac{2}{5}\times 20 = 8\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$. The factor $\frac{2}{5}$ must be applied to $MR^2 = 20$, giving $8$. Forgetting to square the radius, or dropping the $2/5$, leads to the distractor values.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: A uniform disk has mass $M = 4\ \text{kg}$ and radius $R = 3\ \text{m}$. What is its moment of inertia about its central axis?
questions.explanation: A disk has $I = \frac{1}{2}MR^2$. With $R^2 = 9\ \text{m}^2$ and $M = 4\ \text{kg}$, this is $I = \frac{1}{2}\times 4 \times 9 = \frac{1}{2}\times 36 = 18\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$. The full $MR^2 = 36$ would be a hoop of the same size; the disk has half that because its mass is spread inward from the rim. The radius must be squared before halving.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: A flywheel with moment of inertia $I = 8\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$ spins at angular speed $\omega = 3\ \text{rad/s}$. What is its rotational kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Rotational kinetic energy is $T = \tfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2$. With $\omega^2 = 9\ \text{rad}^2/\text{s}^2$ and $I = 8\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$, this gives $T = \tfrac{1}{2}\times 8 \times 9 = 4 \times 9 = 36\ \text{J}$. The angular speed is squared first, then multiplied by the moment of inertia and one half. Using $\omega$ instead of $\omega^2$ would wrongly give $12\ \text{J}$.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: The moment of inertia of a thin uniform spherical shell of mass $M$ and radius $R$ about a diameter is
questions.explanation: A thin spherical shell has $I = \frac{2}{3}MR^2$ about a diameter. All its mass sits at radius $R$, so its coefficient $2/3$ exceeds the $2/5$ of a solid sphere, where mass fills the interior closer to the axis. It is still less than the $MR^2$ of a hoop, because a shell spreads its mass over a sphere rather than concentrating it in a single ring at maximum distance.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: A uniform rod of mass $M = 6\ \text{kg}$ and length $L = 4\ \text{m}$ has moment of inertia $I_{\text{cm}} = 8\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$ about its centre. Using the parallel axis theorem, what is its moment of inertia about a perpendicular axis through one end?
questions.explanation: The end is a distance $d = L/2 = 2\ \text{m}$ from the centre, so $I = I_{\text{cm}} + Md^2 = 8 + 6\times(2)^2 = 8 + 6\times 4 = 8 + 24 = 32\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$. As a check, $\frac{1}{3}ML^2 = \frac{1}{3}\times 6 \times 16 = 32\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$, the known end value. The shift distance is half the length and must be squared in the added term.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: A physical pendulum is a rigid body of mass $M$ swinging about a fixed horizontal axis, with moment of inertia $I$ about that axis and the centre of mass a distance $h$ from it. The angular frequency of small oscillations is
questions.explanation: The restoring torque about the pivot is $-Mgh\sin\theta \approx -Mgh\,\theta$ for small angles, and the equation of motion is $I\ddot{\theta} = -Mgh\,\theta$. This is simple harmonic motion with $\omega^2 = Mgh/I$, so $\omega = \sqrt{Mgh/I}$. The moment of inertia sits in the denominator, since a larger $I$ slows the swing. The inverted form $\sqrt{I/Mgh}$ is the period over $2\pi$, not the frequency.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: A uniform rod of mass $M$ and length $L$ swings as a physical pendulum about a horizontal axis through one end. Using $I = \tfrac{1}{3}ML^2$ and $h = L/2$, the angular frequency of small oscillations is
questions.explanation: Substitute into $\omega = \sqrt{Mgh/I}$: with $h = L/2$ and $I = \frac{1}{3}ML^2$, the ratio is $\frac{Mg(L/2)}{\frac{1}{3}ML^2} = \frac{gL/2}{L^2/3} = \frac{3g}{2L}$. So $\omega = \sqrt{\frac{3g}{2L}}$. The masses cancel, as they must for a pendulum, and the rod swings faster than a simple pendulum of length $L$ because its mass is distributed nearer the pivot. The reciprocal $\sqrt{2g/3L}$ comes from inverting the ratio.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: The principal axes of a rigid body are best described as
options: the axes along which the body is longest
the axes fixed in the laboratory frame
the set of body-fixed axes for which the inertia tensor is diagonal, so that $\mathbf{L}$ is parallel to $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ when the body spins about one of them
any three mutually perpendicular axes through the centre of mass
questions.explanation: The principal axes are the body-fixed directions in which the inertia tensor becomes diagonal; they are its eigenvectors, and the diagonal entries are the principal moments of inertia (the eigenvalues). Spinning about a principal axis makes $\mathbf{L} = I\boldsymbol{\omega}$ point along $\boldsymbol{\omega}$, with no off-diagonal coupling. Not every set of perpendicular axes qualifies; only those that diagonalize $\mathbf{I}$ do, although symmetry axes of a symmetric body are always principal axes.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: A uniform thin hoop has mass $M = 2\ \text{kg}$ and radius $R = 5\ \text{m}$. What is its moment of inertia about the central axis perpendicular to its plane?
questions.explanation: A thin hoop has $I = MR^2$. With $M = 2\ \text{kg}$ and $R^2 = 25\ \text{m}^2$, this is $I = 2 \times 25 = 50\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$. There is no fractional coefficient because every part of the hoop lies at the full radius $R$ from the axis. Using a factor of one half, as for a disk, would wrongly give $25\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: For a torque-free symmetric top (two equal principal moments, $I_1 = I_2 \ne I_3$), Euler's equations show that the spin component $\omega_3$ about the symmetry axis is
options: constant, while $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$ precess steadily about the symmetry axis
questions.explanation: With no torque and $I_1 = I_2$, Euler's third equation becomes $I_3\dot{\omega}_3 - (I_1 - I_2)\omega_1\omega_2 = 0$, and since $I_1 - I_2 = 0$ this gives $\dot{\omega}_3 = 0$, so $\omega_3$ is constant. The remaining two equations then make $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$ rotate at a steady rate, describing the free precession of the angular velocity about the symmetry axis. This is the model for the wobble of a spinning symmetric body such as a tossed disk or a spinning planet.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: Two balls of equal mass and radius, one a solid sphere and one a thin spherical shell, roll without slipping down the same incline from rest. Which reaches the bottom first?
options: They arrive together, since mass and radius are equal
The solid sphere, because its smaller moment of inertia coefficient ($\tfrac{2}{5}$ vs $\tfrac{2}{3}$) puts less energy into rotation, leaving more for translation
The shell, because it has more inertia
It depends only on the radius, which is equal
questions.explanation: Rolling without slipping splits the gravitational energy between translation and rotation. A body with a smaller moment-of-inertia coefficient stores less energy in spin and thus moves faster. The solid sphere's coefficient is $\frac{2}{5}$, less than the shell's $\frac{2}{3}$, so the solid sphere accelerates more and reaches the bottom first. The result is independent of mass and radius, depending only on the shape through that coefficient.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: A uniform solid sphere has mass $M = 5\ \text{kg}$ and radius $R = 2\ \text{m}$. Find its moment of inertia about a diameter.
questions.solution: The solid sphere uses the coefficient {{1}} (as a fraction, write it like 2/5) in $I = cMR^2$. With $R^2 = 4\ \text{m}^2$, the moment of inertia is $I = $ {{2}} kilogram metres squared.
questions.explanation: A solid sphere has $I = \frac{2}{5}MR^2$. With $MR^2 = 5\times 4 = 20$, the result is $I = \frac{2}{5}\times 20 = 8\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: A uniform rod of mass $M = 6\ \text{kg}$ and length $L = 4\ \text{m}$ has $I_{\text{cm}} = 8\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$ about its centre. Use the parallel axis theorem to find the moment of inertia about a perpendicular axis through one end.
questions.solution: The end lies a distance $d = L/2 = $ {{1}} metres from the centre. The parallel axis theorem gives $I = I_{\text{cm}} + Md^2 = 8 + 6\times d^2 = $ {{2}} kilogram metres squared.
questions.explanation: Here $d = 2\ \text{m}$, so $Md^2 = 6\times 4 = 24\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$ and $I = 8 + 24 = 32\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$. This matches the standard end value $\frac{1}{3}ML^2 = \frac{1}{3}\times 6 \times 16 = 32$.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: Match each uniform body to its moment-of-inertia coefficient $c$ in $I = cMR^2$ about the stated axis.
questions.solution: A solid disk about its central axis has $c = $ {{1}} (write like 1/2). A thin hoop about its central axis has $c = $ {{2}}. A solid sphere about a diameter has $c = $ {{3}} (write like 2/5).
questions.explanation: The disk has $c = \frac{1}{2}$, the hoop has $c = 1$ (all mass at the rim), and the solid sphere has $c = \frac{2}{5}$. The coefficient grows as mass moves farther from the axis.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: A flywheel with moment of inertia $I = 8\ \text{kg}\,\text{m}^2$ spins at $\omega = 3\ \text{rad/s}$. Find its rotational kinetic energy.
questions.solution: The rotational kinetic energy is $T = \tfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2$. With $\omega^2 = $ {{1}}, the energy is $T = $ {{2}} joules.
questions.explanation: First $\omega^2 = 3^2 = 9$, then $T = \tfrac{1}{2}\times 8 \times 9 = 36\ \text{J}$. The angular speed must be squared before multiplying by the moment of inertia and one half.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: A uniform rod of mass $M$ and length $L$ swings as a physical pendulum about a horizontal axis through one end. Find the small-oscillation frequency using $I = \tfrac{1}{3}ML^2$ and $h = L/2$.
questions.solution: The physical-pendulum frequency is $\omega = \sqrt{Mgh/I}$. Substituting $h = L/2$ and $I = \tfrac{1}{3}ML^2$, the ratio $Mgh/I$ simplifies to $\dfrac{3g}{2L}$, so $\omega = $ {{1}}. This pendulum swings {{2}} a simple pendulum of length $L$.
questions.explanation: The ratio is $\frac{Mg(L/2)}{ML^2/3} = \frac{3g}{2L}$, so $\omega = \sqrt{3g/(2L)}$. Since $\frac{3}{2} > 1$, this exceeds $\sqrt{g/L}$, so the rod swings faster than a simple pendulum of the same length: its mass sits closer to the pivot on average.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: Describe the inertia tensor and the relation between angular momentum and angular velocity.
questions.solution: The angular momentum is related to the angular velocity by $L_i = \sum_j I_{ij}\omega_j$, where $I_{ij}$ is the inertia {{1}}. In general $\mathbf{L}$ and $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ are {{2}}; they line up only when $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ lies along a {{3}} axis.
questions.explanation: The inertia tensor $I_{ij}$ maps $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ to $\mathbf{L}$. Because it is a matrix, it usually changes direction, so $\mathbf{L}$ and $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ are not parallel except when $\boldsymbol{\omega}$ points along a principal axis (an eigenvector of the tensor).
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics
